title: Forced-Labor Tariffs Replace Expiring 10% Duty as Trump Rebuilds Trade Wall, Dollar Hits 40-Year High on Yen
subhead: The Trump administration rolled out Section 301 tariffs of 10-12.5% on 60 trading partners to replace an expiring global duty, while Brent surged past $100 on Houthi strikes and the dollar hit its strongest against the yen since 1986.

sections.heading: Section 301 Tariffs on 60 Partners Replace Expiring 10% Duty
sections.body:
The Trump administration imposed new tariffs of 10% to 12.5% on goods from 60 trading partners on Friday, replacing the temporary 10% global tariff under Section 122 that expired the same day. The new levies use Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, following a months-long USTR investigation into whether countries enforce bans on forced-labour imports.

Under the structure, countries that have implemented or committed to forced-labour import prohibitions — including Canada, the EU, India, and the UK — face the lower 10% rate. China, Japan, South Korea, and dozens of others that lack such bans face the higher 12.5% rate, though the EU, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, and Switzerland receive partial relief under existing trade pacts. Brazil, separately, faces a 25% tariff on various goods over unfair trade practice allegations. Goods already under sectoral tariffs (steel, aluminum, copper, lumber, autos) are exempt, as are energy products, fertilizers, and USMCA-compliant goods.

USTR Jamieson Greer said the 60 nations covered represent 99.4% of all US imports. Bloomberg Economics estimates the new levies increase the effective US tariff rate by just 0.5 percentage points from its current 10.7%, though a separate Section 301 investigation into excess manufacturing capacity in 16 economies — including China, India, Japan, South Korea, and the EU — could add further duties. Josh Lipsky of the Atlantic Council told AFP the move signals a 'much more protectionist world's largest economy' and that tariff deals remain 'fragile.'
sections.whyItMatters: The Section 301 framework is designed to be legally durable, unlike the emergency-powers tariffs struck down by the Supreme Court in February. This is Trump's permanent tariff wall — and the overcapacity probe means more rounds are coming.

sections.heading: Brent Surges Past $100 as Houthis Strike Saudi Tankers in Red Sea
sections.body:
Brent crude surged above $100 a barrel for the first time since late May, closing at around $100.85 after Houthi forces struck two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea, opening a second front in the disruption of Middle East energy shipping. US crude jumped 6.8% to $92.75. Oil has now risen nearly 40% in July alone.

The Houthi-declared blockade on Saudi Arabia targets the Bab el-Mandeb strait — the 'Gate of Tears' — which controls Red Sea access to the Indian Ocean. Iran had flown Revolutionary Guard commanders and missile equipment into Yemen days before the announcement, according to Reuters sources. The Saudi tanker Encelia transmitted a distress call reporting a missile strike near Jizan; Saudi state media confirmed a fire at the bow.

Insurance costs for shipping through the southern Red Sea doubled for some companies after the attacks, with several tankers rerouting north through the Suez Canal to avoid the Bab el-Mandeb. With the Strait of Hormuz already effectively closed, two of the world's most critical energy chokepoints are now simultaneously disrupted.
sections.whyItMatters: Two major energy chokepoints blocked at once is unprecedented in modern oil markets. The supply squeeze is no longer theoretical — it is being priced into physical freight and insurance in real time.

sections.heading: Dollar Hits 40-Year High vs Yen as US Treasury Warns on Volatility
sections.body:
The dollar strengthened to a fresh 40-year high against the yen at 163.98 on Thursday, closing at 163.89 per dollar on Friday in Asia. The dollar index rose 0.36% to 101.47, its highest level this month, as oil-driven inflation fears boosted expectations of Fed rate hikes while the BOJ remains cautious.

Markets now price a 35.8% chance of a 25bp Fed hike next week, up from 11.8% a week ago, and an 81.4% probability of a September hike, up from 52.4%, according to CME FedWatch. The initial jobless claims figure of 187,000 — the largest decline in three months and well below the 212,000 estimate — reinforced the view of a resilient US economy that can withstand tighter policy.

The US Treasury Department warned that excessive yen volatility is undesirable and called for the Bank of Japan to raise rates. Japan's finance minister Satsuki Katayama reiterated readiness for decisive forex intervention after yen-buying operations in April and May. Goldman Sachs expects the BOJ to keep rates unchanged at its July meeting, with the next hike likely in January. IG analyst Tony Sycamore said supporting the yen now would be 'akin to standing in the way of a bullet train.' Japan's 2-year government bond yield hit a 31-year high on bets of accelerated BOJ tightening.
sections.whyItMatters: The dollar-yen gap is being driven by divergent central bank expectations: surging oil prices push the Fed toward hikes while the BOJ drags its feet. US Treasury pressure on Japan signals growing friction over currency policy.

sections.heading: ECB Holds Rates Unanimously, Leaves September Door Wide Open
sections.body:
The ECB left all three key rates unchanged on Thursday — deposit facility at 2.25%, main refinancing at 2.40%, marginal lending at 2.65% — following last month's 25bp hike. The decision was unanimous, President Christine Lagarde confirmed, with the Governing Council noting that while underlying inflation has been 'contained,' the full effects of the energy shock have yet to play out.

Euro area inflation fell to 2.8% in June from 3.2% in May, with energy prices up 8.5% year-on-year — down from 10.8% in both April and May but certain to rise again as Brent's move above $100 feeds through. Markets now price a 71% chance of an ECB rate hike in September, according to LSEG data.

ING's Carsten Brzeski argued the June inflation decline and absence of second-round effects should have reduced the case for a hike, but added: 'Unless oil prices start dropping significantly over the next weeks, the ECB's own macro projections in September will call for another rate hike, loud and clear.' Morgan Stanley noted a September hike would be positive for the euro, as markets are not pricing in enough restriction to hurt the currency.
sections.whyItMatters: The ECB is in a holding pattern, but the September repricing is the real story. With oil back above $100 and showing no sign of falling, Lagarde's 'meeting-by-meeting' approach masks a likely September hike that will compound the euro zone's growth headwinds.

sections.heading: House Passes War Powers Resolution, Senate Blocks It
sections.body:
The US House of Representatives passed a war powers resolution directing Trump to withdraw from the Iran conflict, by a 214-208 vote — largely along party lines, with the same four Republicans (Barrett, Davidson, Massie, Fitzpatrick) breaking ranks as in June. Representative Pramila Jayapal, the resolution's sponsor, called it a bipartisan statement that 'vast majorities of Americans believe this is an unconstitutional, illegal war.' The war has now cost 18 American service members' lives, she noted.

Hours later, the Senate rejected a parallel resolution from Democrat Chris Van Hollen in a 47-49 vote. The House measure is largely symbolic — the Trump administration has argued war powers resolutions are unconstitutional — but the recurring Republican defections signal growing political costs of the conflict ahead of November midterms.

The vote came on the same day as the 13th consecutive night of US strikes on Iran, with Iran firing back at US bases in Jordan and Kuwait, and oil prices surging past $100. The political calculus is shifting: rising fuel costs and casualty figures are pressuring Republican lawmakers in competitive districts.
sections.whyItMatters: Congress is failing to constrain the war, but the 214-208 House vote and four Republican defections signal eroding party discipline. With oil above $100 and midterms approaching, the political cost of the Iran conflict is climbing.

sections.heading: South Korea Fights for 15% Tariff Cap as Asia Markets Slump
sections.body:
Seoul's presidential office said Friday it would consult closely with Washington to ensure that new Section 301 tariffs do not push Korean goods above the 15% rate agreed in their November 2025 trade deal. South Korea, which pledged $350 billion in US investments to secure that rate cut, was placed in a 'tariff top-up' group alongside Japan and Switzerland — meaning goods with existing MFN rates below 12.5% will face a supplemental duty to bring the combined rate to 12.5%.

A separate Section 301 investigation into excess manufacturing capacity in 16 economies, including South Korea, could add a second layer of duties — a more pressing concern for Seoul than the forced-labour tariffs themselves. Korean Trade Ministers Kim Jung-kwan and Yeo Han-koo made urgent visits to Washington this week, meeting Commerce Secretary Lutnick and USTR Greer respectively.

Asian markets absorbed the combined shock of tariffs and surging oil. South Korea's Kospi dropped 3.7%, Japan's Nikkei slid 2.9%, and MSCI's Asia-Pacific index outside Japan fell 1%. The Singapore dollar and Malaysian ringgit came under pressure as both countries face the new 10% tariff, with Singapore's foreign minister calling the tariffs 'unjustified' and warning against becoming 'collateral damage.' Singapore's MAS is expected to keep monetary policy unchanged at its review next week as core inflation accelerated to 1.6% in June on higher energy costs.
sections.whyItMatters: The Section 301 tariffs hit Asia hardest — and the overcapacity investigation threatens a second wave. Countries that signed trade deals with Washington are finding those deals provide less protection than expected.

sections.heading: Bond Yields Surge, Gold Slips as Inflation Fears Resurface
sections.body:
The US 10-year Treasury yield hit an 18-month high of 4.703%, while the 30-year yield reached 5.17%, just below a 19-year peak. European borrowing costs climbed to highs not seen since 2011. The yield surge reflects a repricing of central bank expectations: markets have gone from pricing negligible rate hikes a week ago to a one-in-three chance of a Fed move next week.

Gold fell 2% to $4,043/oz as the dollar rally drew safe-haven flows away from bullion. Silver dropped 3.4% to $57.45/oz. The dollar's strength reflects its perceived insulation from energy price shocks relative to Europe and Japan, combined with the repricing of Fed hike probabilities.

Oil's 40% July surge is reviving inflation expectations that had appeared to be easing. As deVere Group CEO Nigel Green put it: 'This looks less like a short-lived spike and more like a genuine reopening of the inflation question.' The term premium embedded in long-dated Treasuries reflects the market's assessment that supply-side shocks from dual-chokepoint disruption are unlikely to be transitory.
sections.whyItMatters: The bond market is signaling that the inflation reprieve of recent months is over. With 10-year yields at 18-month highs and 30-year yields near 19-year peaks, the cost of capital is rising just as growth headwinds from tariffs and energy shocks intensify.

bottomLine: Thursday and Friday marked a convergence of trade, monetary, and geopolitical pressure. The Trump administration replaced an expiring 10% global tariff with legally sturdier Section 301 duties on 60 countries — a tariff wall designed to survive court challenges and expand through the overcapacity investigation. Brent oil's surge above $100 on Houthi strikes on Saudi tankers is feeding directly into central bank calculus: the ECB kept rates unchanged but opened the door to a September hike, Fed hike expectations doubled in a week, and the dollar hit a 40-year high against the yen even as the US Treasury warned Tokyo about excessive weakness. Congress tried and failed to rein in the Iran war. The net effect: higher financing costs, higher input costs, and higher tariffs — all at once.
sources: Reuters, CNBC, Xinhua, USA Today, AP, Straits Times, Jamaica Observer/AFP, Korea Herald, News18/Reuters, Firstpost, Bloomberg, MSN/Reuters, LSEG, CME FedWatch, Atlantic Council, deVere Group, ING Research, Morgan Stanley, Goldman Sachs, IG, Bloomberg Economics, USTR
